description: The kernels that power modern large language models: softmax, layer norm, attention, FlashAttention algorithm, KV cache, quantization, and Hopper-specific optimizations (TMA, warpgroup MMA). Synthesizes all prior clusters in production-grade implementations.

concepts.title: GPU Softmax Kernel
concepts.preciseDefinition: softmax(x)_i = exp(x_i - max(x)) / sum_j(exp(x_j - max(x))). Numerically stable implementation requires two passes: (1) find max, (2) compute exp and sum. The online softmax algorithm (Milakov & Gimelshein, 2018) fuses both passes into one, reducing memory traffic from 2N reads to ~1.5N reads by maintaining running max and sum simultaneously. Used in attention score normalization.
keyFacts: Naive 3-pass: read x for max → read x for exp/sum → read exp for normalize = 3N reads, 1N write
Online softmax: 1 pass maintaining (m_i, d_i) running stats — 1N reads for stats + 1N reads for output = 2 passes total
Numerical stability: subtract max before exp to prevent overflow (exp(x - max) ≤ 1)
Per-row softmax for attention: each row of Q@K^T scores normalized independently → each warp handles one or more rows
Flash Attention key insight: online softmax enables chunked attention without materializing full attention matrix
commonMisconceptions: Numerically stable softmax requires computing max in a separate pass — online softmax computes max and sum simultaneously
Softmax is always memory-bound — for small sequence lengths (< 512), compute can be significant
Subtracting max changes the result — exp(x_i - max) / sum(exp(x_j - max)) = exp(x_i) / sum(exp(x_j)) mathematically

concepts.title: Online Softmax Algorithm
concepts.preciseDefinition: An algorithm that computes softmax in a single pass over input data by maintaining two running statistics: m_i (running maximum) and d_i (running normalized sum). When processing element x_j: m_i_new = max(m_i, x_j); d_i_new = d_i × exp(m_i - m_i_new) + exp(x_j - m_i_new). The correction factor exp(m_i - m_i_new) rescales previous partial sums when a new maximum is found. First described by Milakov & Gimelshein (2018).
keyFacts: Only one pass over the data: running update of (max, rescaled_sum) statistics
Memory traffic: 1 pass read + 1 pass write (vs 3 passes for naive) = 2× bandwidth saving
FlashAttention extends online softmax to the attention mechanism: tile over key/value blocks maintaining (m, l) statistics
Rescaling previous partial output O_i: O_i_new = exp(m_i - m_i_new) × O_i + exp(q_i @ k_j - m_i_new) × v_j
log-sum-exp (LSE) = m + log(d) is stored for use in the backward pass
commonMisconceptions: Online softmax produces approximate results — it is numerically exact (within floating point precision)
Online softmax requires more computation than 2-pass — it requires a correction multiply per step but saves memory passes
Online softmax only works for vector inputs — it extends to chunked computation across blocks in FlashAttention

exercises.title: Fused Online Softmax Kernel
exercises.whatItTeaches: 3-pass naïve softmax → online single-pass softmax; why reduction followed by exp+sum is 3× more reads; implementing running (max, sum) statistics with warp shuffle; per-row parallelism with one warp per row.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // Each warp handles one row. Phase 1: reduce max with __shfl_down_sync. Phase 2: online_softmax maintaining (m, d) pair. // float m = -INF, d = 0; for each x_j: float m_new = fmaxf(m, x_j); d = d * expf(m - m_new) + expf(x_j - m_new); m = m_new;
exercises.testCaseHint: Test on rows of length 64, 512, 1024, 4096; compare numerical output against torch.softmax; benchmark vs PyTorch CUDA softmax

concepts.title: Layer Normalization Kernel
concepts.preciseDefinition: LayerNorm(x) = γ × (x - μ) / √(σ² + ε) + β, where μ and σ² are computed over the feature dimension (not batch). Implementation: (1) compute mean μ = (1/D)Σx_i, (2) compute variance σ² = (1/D)Σ(x_i - μ)², (3) normalize. Welford's online algorithm computes mean and variance in a single pass. Memory-bound for typical LLM hidden sizes (D=2048-16384).
keyFacts: Two-pass: pass 1 computes mean, pass 2 computes variance and normalizes — 3 reads, 1 write
Welford's one-pass: maintain running mean and M2 (sum of squared deviations) — 1 read, 1 write
Parallelism: one block per sequence position (row), threads reduce over hidden dimension
D=4096: 16KB of FP32 data per row — fits in L1 cache for reuse across weight γ and β application
RMSNorm: simplified variant omitting mean subtraction — used in LLaMA family; slightly faster
commonMisconceptions: LayerNorm is compute-bound for large D — it is memory-bound for all practical hidden sizes (D < 32768)
Fusing γ and β into the normalization is complex — they are simple pointwise multiplies after normalization
LayerNorm and BatchNorm differ only in axes — LayerNorm normalizes over features, making behavior independent of batch size

exercises.title: Fused LayerNorm + Residual
exercises.whatItTeaches: Fusing residual addition with LayerNorm computation; Welford's online algorithm for one-pass mean+variance; why fusion eliminates an intermediate global memory write.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // Fused: x = residual + x_in; then layernorm(x). Single kernel reads residual+x_in once, writes normalized output. // Welford update: n++; float delta = x - mean; mean += delta/n; float delta2 = x - mean; M2 += delta * delta2;
exercises.testCaseHint: Compare against separate residual+layernorm; verify numerical equivalence; measure bandwidth reduction from fusion

concepts.title: Scaled Dot-Product Attention
concepts.preciseDefinition: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k) × V, where Q,K,V ∈ ℝ^{N×d_k}. Naive implementation: 2 GEMM (QK^T then ×V) + 1 softmax. For sequence length N, FLOPs = 4N²d_k; memory = 4Nd_k + N² (attention matrix). Arithmetic intensity = 4N²d_k / (4Nd_k + N²) ≈ N for large N — becomes memory-bound when N² attention matrix must be read/written to HBM.
keyFacts: Standard attention materializes N×N attention matrix — O(N²) memory cost prohibits long sequences
Multi-head attention: h independent attention heads; Q,K,V split to d_k = d_model/h per head
GQA (Grouped Query Attention): K and V have fewer heads (groups) than Q — used in LLaMA-2/3, Mistral
MQA (Multi-Query Attention): single K and V head; extreme GQA; fastest KV cache but accuracy tradeoff
KV cache: store K and V for previously processed tokens; O(N × d_k × 2) memory per layer per token
commonMisconceptions: Attention is compute-bound like GEMM — attention is memory-bound for long sequences due to N² attention matrix reads
Multi-head attention is slower than single-head — batched GEMM makes it equivalent to single GEMM
KV cache size is fixed — it grows linearly with context length; critical for long-context inference

concepts.title: FlashAttention: IO-Aware Attention
concepts.preciseDefinition: A tiled attention algorithm (Dao et al., 2022) that computes exact attention without materializing the N×N attention matrix in HBM. Tiles Q, K, V blocks into SRAM, uses online softmax to accumulate partial results, and writes only the final O output to HBM. HBM reads: O(Nd_k); standard attention: O(N²). Reduces HBM transfers by O(N/M) where M is SRAM size (~80-228 KB on H100).
keyFacts: FA1 (2022): tiled forward pass, recompute attention weights in backward (no N² storage)
FA2 (2023): better work partitioning (parallelism over sequence length), fewer non-matmul FLOPs
FA3 (2024): Hopper-specific — WGMMA, TMA, pingpong pipelining, FP8 forward; 1.5-2.0× vs FA2 on H100
FA4 (2025): CuTeDSL, SM90+SM100, supports Blackwell 2CTA, GQA packing, paged KV, block sparsity
Key constraint: Q, K, V block must fit in SRAM — block size BQ × BK × d_k × 2 (Q+K) ≤ SRAM
commonMisconceptions: FlashAttention is an approximate algorithm — it computes exact attention, just reorders operations for IO efficiency
FlashAttention is always faster than standard attention — only for N > threshold (~512 on H100); smaller N: standard attention has lower overhead
FlashAttention backward requires storing the attention matrix — backward recomputes attention weights on-the-fly using stored LSE values

concepts.title: FlashAttention Backward Pass
concepts.preciseDefinition: The FlashAttention backward pass (Dao et al., 2022, Algorithm 4) avoids storing the N×N attention matrix by recomputing it from Q, K during the backward pass using stored log-sum-exp (LSE) values. Gradients dQ, dK, dV are computed by loading Q, K, V tiles and recomputing softmax(QK^T/√d) on-the-fly. HBM reads: O(N×d_k) for both forward and backward.
keyFacts: Recomputation strategy: store Q, K, V (O(Nd)) + LSE (O(N)) instead of attention weights (O(N²))
For d=128, N=65536: attention matrix = 16 GB; recomputation overhead ≈ 2× FLOPs of forward
dV requires: Σ_i A_ij dO_i — one additional pass over (Q, K) to recompute A for each K/V block
dQ, dK can be computed simultaneously by iterating over Q and K tiles
FA3 backward on H100: achieves ~50% of forward throughput
commonMisconceptions: FlashAttention backward is slower than forward because of recomputation — the extra FLOPs are negligible vs HBM bandwidth savings
Stored activations for backward are the attention weights — FlashAttention stores only LSE (N scalars), not N×N matrix

exercises.title: Flash Attention Forward (From Scratch)
exercises.whatItTeaches: Implementing the core FlashAttention tiling loop: outer loop over Q blocks, inner loop over K/V blocks, online softmax update with rescaling of running sum O_i, writing final normalized output. Understanding why IO complexity is O(N) not O(N²).
exercises.starterCodeHint: // For each Q tile: load Q to SRAM // For each K,V tile: S = Q @ K^T / sqrt(d); m_new = max(m, rowmax(S)); l_new = exp(m-m_new)*l + rowsum(exp(S-m_new)); O = (exp(m-m_new)*O + exp(S-m_new) @ V) / l_new;
exercises.testCaseHint: Compare against naive attention (materialize N×N) for seq_len=512, d=64, batch=4; verify max abs error < 1e-4; benchmark memory traffic reduction vs naive

concepts.title: KV Cache for Autoregressive Decoding
concepts.preciseDefinition: During autoregressive token generation, each new token attends to all previous tokens' keys and values. Recomputing K and V for all previous tokens each step is O(N²) total. KV cache stores all previously computed K and V tensors; each new token only computes K, V for its own position and appends to cache. Memory: O(batch × layers × seq_len × 2 × d_k × dtype_size). For LLaMA-3 70B at FP16: ~2 GB for seq_len=8192.
keyFacts: KV cache size for LLaMA-3 70B (fp16, seq=8192): 32 layers × 32 heads × 8192 × 128 × 2 × 2B = 4 GB
Paged KV cache (vLLM): KV blocks managed like virtual memory pages to avoid fragmentation
GQA reduces KV cache size by ratio num_kv_heads/num_q_heads: LLaMA-3 70B uses 8 KV heads vs 64 Q heads (8× smaller)
Prefill (prompt processing): compute all K, V at once via batched GEMM
Decode (token generation): K, V for new token added; attention is GEMV (matrix-vector), not GEMM — memory-bound
commonMisconceptions: KV cache eliminates the need to store model weights — KV cache is additional memory on top of model weights
Decode attention with KV cache is compute-bound — it is a GEMV (vector × matrix), always memory-bound
Longer context requires proportionally longer decode latency — with KV cache, each step is O(1) in computation

concepts.title: INT8 and FP8 Quantization Kernels
concepts.preciseDefinition: Quantization maps FP32/FP16 weights and activations to lower-precision integers or low-bit floats. INT8 linear quantization: x_q = round(clamp(x / scale, -128, 127)); dequantize: x = x_q × scale. FP8 (E4M3/E5M2) uses hardware-native 8-bit floats available on Hopper and Blackwell. Key challenge: calibrating scales to minimize accuracy loss; per-channel vs per-tensor granularity tradeoff.
keyFacts: INT8 GEMM on H100 via cuBLAS CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32I or CUTLASS: 2× throughput vs INT16; ~4× vs FP32
FP8 E4M3: max value 448, best for weights (lower range needed); E5M2: max value 57344, for gradients
Per-channel quantization: one scale per output channel of weight — better accuracy than per-tensor
Dynamic quantization: compute scale per-batch at runtime; static: pre-calibrated on dataset
AWQ, GPTQ, SmoothQuant: popular techniques for accurate INT4/INT8 LLM quantization
Dequantization fused into GEMM epilogue via CUTLASS EVT to avoid extra memory pass
commonMisconceptions: INT8 quantization always halves memory — only true for weights; activations may remain FP16
Quantization is just rounding — accuracy-preserving quantization requires careful scale calibration and may require weight reordering
FP8 and INT8 require the same programming approach — FP8 uses native hardware arithmetic; INT8 needs explicit dequantization

exercises.title: INT8 Quantize/Dequantize Kernel
exercises.whatItTeaches: Per-tensor and per-channel quantization; absmax scaling; saturating cast to INT8; vectorized load/store with char4; fusion of dequantization into activation functions.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // Per-tensor: float scale = absmax / 127.f; int8_t q = (int8_t)round(clamp(x / scale, -128, 127)); // Vectorized: process 4 bytes at once with char4
exercises.testCaseHint: Verify round-trip accuracy (quantize then dequantize) within scale/2 for uniform distribution; test saturation at boundaries

concepts.title: Kernel Fusion
concepts.preciseDefinition: Combining multiple independent operations into a single GPU kernel to eliminate intermediate reads/writes to global memory. For memory-bound kernels, fusion converts multiple bandwidth-bound passes into one. Example: fusing GELU + bias into the output of a linear layer eliminates an additional O(N) memory read. The arithmetic intensity of the fused kernel is the sum of each operation's FLOPs divided by total bytes touched (which is ≤ sum of individual bytes).
keyFacts: Fusing N memory-bound ops reduces HBM traffic from N×X to X bytes (where X is shared input/output size)
Common fusions in LLMs: LayerNorm+Linear, GELU+bias, attention+softmax+dropout, residual+layernorm
CUDA Graphs: capture a sequence of kernel launches as a graph for replay with reduced launch overhead
Torch.compile (Triton-based) and cuDNN graph fusion auto-fuse ops — manual fusion needed for maximum control
FlashAttention is the canonical example: fuses QK^T, softmax, and @V into one kernel eliminating O(N²) memory traffic
commonMisconceptions: Fusion always improves performance — if each op is compute-bound, fusion does not improve throughput
Fusion increases register pressure significantly — depends on how many live variables; careful design mitigates this
CUDA Graphs and kernel fusion are the same thing — CUDA Graphs reduce launch overhead; fusion reduces per-kernel memory traffic

exercises.title: Fused GELU + Bias Addition
exercises.whatItTeaches: Implementing GELU activation (tanh approximation) fused with bias addition; vectorized float4 loads for GELU; measuring memory bandwidth improvement vs unfused version.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // GELU(x) = 0.5f * x * (1.0f + tanhf(SQRT_2_PI * (x + 0.044715f * x*x*x))); // Fuse: y = GELU(x + bias)
exercises.testCaseHint: Compare against torch.nn.GELU for correctness; benchmark bandwidth at N=10M; show 2× read reduction vs separate bias+gelu kernels

concepts.title: Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA)
concepts.preciseDefinition: A Hopper (H100, cc9.0) hardware unit that offloads multi-dimensional memory copy coordination from the GPU's scalar pipelines to a dedicated hardware engine. Instead of programming explicit loops for tiled copy, the programmer describes the copy via a TMA descriptor (shape, stride, swizzle) and issues a single TMA instruction (cp.async.bulk.tensor). Supports 1D-5D tensor copies, reduction, and multicast to Thread Block Clusters.
keyFacts: TMA descriptor created on host with cuTensorMapEncodeIm2col / cuTensorMapEncodeTiled
TMA copies: cp.async.bulk.tensor.{1..5}d — asynchronous, uses mbarrier for completion signaling
TMA eliminates scalar address computation overhead: ~32 threads × 4 cycles vs 1 TMA instruction
TMA multicast: H100 sends data to multiple blocks in a Thread Block Cluster simultaneously
TMA swizzle: reorders bytes to avoid shared memory bank conflicts automatically
FlashAttention 3 uses TMA for async prefetching of K/V tiles while previous tile is being processed
commonMisconceptions: TMA is just async copy (cp.async from Ampere) — TMA is a separate hardware engine with a descriptor; cp.async is warp-issued
TMA works on all CUDA compute capabilities — requires cc9.0+ (Hopper)
TMA automatically handles tiled copying for any layout — requires the programmer to set up the TMA descriptor correctly

concepts.title: Warpgroup MMA (WGMMA)
concepts.preciseDefinition: A Hopper (cc9.0) MMA instruction that operates on 4 warps simultaneously (a warpgroup of 128 threads). WGMMA tiles are larger than single-warp WMMA: e.g., wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n256k16.f32.bf16.bf16 processes a 64×256×16 MMA. The 'async' variant allows non-blocking execution and is the mechanism behind Hopper's compute/memory pipelining. Exposed via CUTLASS cute::WGMMA or inline PTX.
keyFacts: WGMMA operates on 4 warps (warpgroup); requires threadblock of exactly 128 threads per warpgroup
Tile shapes for H100: m64n{8..256}k16 (BF16/FP16); m64n{8..256}k32 (FP8)
WGMMA async: non-blocking — other warps can execute while WGMMA runs in background (warp specialization)
wgmma.fence / wgmma.commit_group / wgmma.wait_group: pipeline barriers for chaining WGMMA operations
FlashAttention 3 uses warp specialization: producer warps load K/V via TMA; consumer warps execute WGMMA
commonMisconceptions: WGMMA is just WMMA for 4 warps in a loop — WGMMA is a fundamentally different instruction class enabling async pipelining
WGMMA can be used with any thread block size — requires exactly 4 warps per warpgroup (128 threads)
WGMMA exposes the same SASS as WMMA — WGMMA generates HGMMA SASS instructions, not HMMA

concepts.title: FlashAttention IO Complexity Analysis
concepts.preciseDefinition: FlashAttention (Dao et al. 2022, Theorem 2) proves that computing exact attention requires Θ(N²d²M⁻¹) HBM accesses, where N is sequence length, d is head dimension, and M is SRAM size. Standard attention requires Θ(Nd + N²) HBM accesses (dominated by the N×N attention matrix). Since typically d² ≪ M (d=128, M≈100KB → d²=16384 ≪ M), FlashAttention achieves up to 9× fewer HBM reads/writes vs. standard attention. Proposition 3 proves this is optimal: no exact attention algorithm can do better asymptotically.
keyFacts: Standard attention: Θ(Nd + N²) HBM accesses — the N² term dominates for long sequences
FlashAttention: Θ(N²d²M⁻¹) HBM accesses — for d=64, M=100KB, this is ~4× fewer accesses
Optimality lower bound: Proposition 3 proves no exact attention algorithm beats Θ(N²d²M⁻¹) HBM accesses for all M
Memory: FlashAttention requires O(N) additional memory (storing LSE statistics), not O(N²)
Despite higher FLOPs (recomputation), FA is faster due to drastically lower HBM traffic (bandwidth is bottleneck)
commonMisconceptions: Reducing FLOPs always makes kernels faster — FlashAttention has more FLOPs than standard attention but is faster due to lower HBM traffic
O(N) memory means N elements stored — FlashAttention stores O(N) statistics (m, l per row) not the O(N²) attention matrix
The IO complexity bound is loose — Theorem 2 is tight: block sizes are chosen optimally as Bc = ⌈4M/d⌉, Br = min(⌈4M/d⌉, d)

concepts.title: SRAM Tiling Strategy in FlashAttention
concepts.preciseDefinition: FlashAttention Algorithm 1 (FA1 §3.1) sets block sizes Bc = ⌈4M/d⌉ and Br = min(⌈4M/d⌉, d) to ensure each K/V/Q block fits in SRAM of size M. The outer loop iterates over Tc = ⌈N/Bc⌉ KV blocks (loaded once), the inner loop over Tr = ⌈N/Br⌉ Q blocks. For each (i,j) pair: load Q_i, K_j, V_j into SRAM; compute S_ij = Q_i × K_j^T; update running (m_i, l_i, O_i) statistics using online softmax. This avoids ever materializing the full N×N attention matrix.
keyFacts: Block sizes derived from SRAM size M: Bc = ⌈4M/d⌉ ensures one K/V block = 2 × Bc × d floats ≤ M
Each SRAM block holds Q_i (Br×d), K_j (Bc×d), V_j (Bc×d), S_ij (Br×Bc), O_i (Br×d) — total ≤ M
Outer loop over KV blocks (j=1..Tc): load K_j, V_j once; inner loop over Q blocks (i=1..Tr)
FA2 swaps loop order (outer=Q, inner=KV) to enable sequence-length parallelism; FA1 has outer=KV, inner=Q
For H100 SRAM = 228 KB, d=128: Bc ≈ 228KB/(4×128×4B) ≈ 112; practical FA uses Bc=64-128
commonMisconceptions: Tiling order in FA1 vs FA2 is equivalent for performance — FA2 swapping inner/outer loops enables crucial sequence parallelism
Block size choice is arbitrary — block sizes must be chosen so the working set fits in SRAM; too large = slow SRAM spill
All four matrices (Q,K,V,O) are kept in SRAM simultaneously — only the blocks for one (i,j) pair reside in SRAM at once

concepts.title: Backward Pass Recomputation in FlashAttention
concepts.preciseDefinition: The FlashAttention backward pass (FA1 Algorithm 4, FA2 Algorithm 2) avoids storing the N×N attention matrix P by recomputing it from Q, K during the backward pass using stored log-sum-exp (LSE) values L_i = m_i + log(l_i). For each backward pass tile, the kernel reloads Q_i, K_j and recomputes P_ij = exp(S_ij − L_i) on-chip, then immediately uses it to accumulate dV, dK, dQ gradients. This selective recomputation trades 33% more FLOPs for O(N²) → O(N) backward memory.
keyFacts: Stored from forward pass: Q, K, V (O(Nd) total) + LSE L_i per row (O(N)) — no N×N storage
FA1 Algorithm 4 stores (m, l) separately; FA2 Algorithm 2 stores only L = m + log(l), saving memory
Recomputation overhead: ~33% extra FLOPs in backward vs. forward — dwarfed by HBM bandwidth savings
For N=65536, d=128: avoiding attention matrix storage saves 16 GB; cost is recomputing ~2× forward FLOPs
FA3 backward on H100 achieves ~50% of forward pass throughput despite recomputation overhead
commonMisconceptions: Recomputation in FA is the same as gradient checkpointing — FA recomputation is per-tile, within one kernel; checkpointing is per-layer
Backward recomputation doubles the runtime — the extra compute is hidden behind the dominant memory bandwidth savings
FA backward needs all of Q,K,V in SRAM simultaneously — backward processes tiles the same way as forward

concepts.title: FA2: Sequence-Length Parallelism
concepts.preciseDefinition: FlashAttention-2 (FA2 §3.2) adds parallelization over the sequence length dimension in addition to FA1's batch×heads parallelism. FA1 assigned one thread block per attention head. FA2 assigns one thread block per (batch, head, query_tile), so the outer query loop becomes embarrassingly parallel. For long sequences with small batch sizes (common in LLM inference), this additional dimension fills idle SMs. FA2 forward achieves 50-73% of theoretical max TFLOPs vs. FA1's 30-50%.
keyFacts: FA1 parallelism: batch × n_heads thread blocks; FA2 adds Tr = ⌈N/Br⌉ more blocks per head
At N=8192, Br=64: Tr=128; for batch=2, heads=32: FA2 schedules 2×32×128=8192 thread blocks vs. FA1's 64
Sequence parallelism is especially valuable for inference (batch=1) where FA1's 64 blocks leave most SMs idle
Backward pass also parallelized over sequence length; uses atomic adds for dQ updates across thread blocks
FA2 reaches up to 225 TFLOPs/s per A100 GPU for GPT-style training (72% MFU)
commonMisconceptions: FA2 requires different CUDA code than FA1 — FA2 is the same algorithm with loop order swapped and added thread block dimensions
Sequence-length parallelism was invented in FA2 — Phil Tillet first implemented this idea in Triton (cited in FA2 paper)
Parallelizing over sequence length always helps — only beneficial when batch×heads is too small to fill the GPU

concepts.title: FA2: Warp Work Partitioning
concepts.preciseDefinition: Within each thread block, FA2 (§3.3) changes how work is distributed across 4 warps. FA1 used 'split-K': K and V split across 4 warps, Q broadcast — each warp computes a slice of QK^T, then must communicate partial sums via shared memory to complete the output. FA2 instead splits Q across 4 warps while keeping K and V accessible by all — each warp computes its slice of QK^T and multiplies directly with V without inter-warp communication. This eliminates shared memory synchronization overhead.
keyFacts: FA1 'split-K': 4 warps each hold K/V shard → 4 partial QK^T → write to smem → sync → reduce → matmul V
FA2 'split-Q': 4 warps each hold Q shard → independent QK^T → independent @V → no inter-warp communication
Eliminating the smem sync reduces shared memory reads/writes by ~2× in the forward pass
FA2 backward also avoids split-K: warps hold K/V shards for dK/dV, avoiding dQ reduction overhead
This change alone accounts for ~1.5× speedup within a thread block vs. FA1
commonMisconceptions: Warp work partitioning is a hardware detail — it is a software design choice with measurable performance impact
Split-K is always better than split-Q — split-K is better for GEMM with small N; split-Q is better for attention's inner loop
FA2's warp partitioning is unique to CUDA — Triton implements the same idea via axis-parallel tiling

concepts.title: Causal Masking Block Skipping
concepts.preciseDefinition: In autoregressive attention, entries S_ij with j > i are masked to −∞, making the upper triangle of the attention matrix all zeros after softmax. FA2 (§3.1.1) exploits this by skipping entire blocks where all column indices exceed all row indices — about half the blocks for large N. Only the diagonal blocks (where the triangle cuts through) need masking applied element-wise. This yields 1.7-1.8× speedup for causal attention vs. non-causal attention of the same size.
keyFacts: For block tile (i,j): skip entirely if min_col_idx(j) > max_row_idx(i) — approximately N²/2 blocks skipped
Only ~N/Br diagonal blocks need element-wise masking applied; remaining lower-triangular blocks computed without masking
1.7-1.8× speedup vs. non-causal attention at large N (since ~50% of work is skipped)
FA3 inherits this optimization; TMA loads can be skipped for masked-out KV blocks
Combined with FA2 sequence parallelism, efficient causal attention enables training at N=8192+ on H100
commonMisconceptions: Causal masking adds significant overhead — block-level skipping makes causal ~2× faster vs. non-causal at large N
Masking must be applied element-wise to all blocks — only the diagonal blocks crossing the causal boundary need masking
Causal masking changes the FA algorithm — it is an optimization pass on top of the same tiling algorithm

concepts.title: FA3: WGMMA Usage on Hopper
concepts.preciseDefinition: FlashAttention-3 (FA3 §3.1) replaces Ampere HMMA with Hopper's asynchronous WGMMA (warpgroup-wide matrix multiply-accumulate). Unlike HMMA, WGMMA is non-blocking: the warpgroup can issue the next instruction before the current matmul finishes. FA3 uses two WGMMA variants per iteration: SS-GEMM (shared memory × shared memory) for QK^T computation, and RS-GEMM (register × shared memory) for PV computation. Warp-specialization splits the 128-thread CTA into producer warps (TMA loads) and consumer warps (WGMMA execution).
keyFacts: WGMMA is asynchronous: issuing TMA does not stall on WGMMA completion; pipelining is explicit via commit/wait
Producer warpgroup: issues TMA loads of K_j, V_j with s-stage circular SMEM buffer; uses setmaxnreg to free registers
Consumer warpgroup: executes WGMMA SS-GEMM and RS-GEMM; reallocates registers freed by producer
FA3 FP16 forward achieves 740 TFLOPs/s (75% H100 utilization) vs FA2's ~35% utilization
FA3 backward achieves 1.5-1.75× speedup vs FA2 backward on H100
commonMisconceptions: WGMMA is available on A100 (Ampere) — WGMMA is Hopper-only (cc9.0+); Ampere uses HMMA
FA3 only benefits from WGMMA — the full benefit comes from WGMMA + TMA + pingpong scheduling together
Warp-specialization requires a different programming model — it uses the same CUDA C++ with setmaxnreg PTX

concepts.title: FA3: TMA-Based Async Pipeline
concepts.preciseDefinition: FA3 (§3.2) implements a 2-stage WGMMA-softmax pipeline that overlaps GEMM with softmax within one consumer warpgroup. In iteration j: WGMMA computes Q_i × K_j^T (GEMM1, non-blocking); while GEMM1 runs asynchronously, softmax stats (m_i, P̃_i) are computed from the previous iteration's S (iteration j-1); then WGMMA computes P̃_{j-1} × V_{j-1} (GEMM2, non-blocking). Since the exponential (softmax) and matmul use separate hardware units (multi-function unit vs Tensor Core), they can run concurrently, hiding the 256× lower throughput of special functions.
keyFacts: H100 has 989 TFLOPS FP16 matmul but only 3.9 TFLOPS special functions (exp) — 256× disparity
2-stage pipeline: GEMM1 (QK^T) of iteration j overlapped with softmax of iteration j-1 (using separate hardware units)
Pingpong scheduling: two consumer warpgroups alternate GEMMs and softmax to double utilization
Register pressure cost: 2-stage pipeline requires extra Br×Bc registers for Snext; 3-stage needs even more
Practical gain: 2-stage pipeline improves from 570 TFLOPs to 620-640 TFLOPs for FP16 forward with d=128
commonMisconceptions: Softmax and GEMM cannot be overlapped because of data dependencies — FA3 pipelines across iterations to break this dependency
The pipeline requires special hardware — it uses standard WGMMA async and mbarrier synchronization primitives
More pipeline stages always improve performance — 3-stage is register-hungry and may not beat 2-stage for all block sizes

concepts.title: FA3: FP8 Quantized Attention
concepts.preciseDefinition: FA3 (§3.3) supports FP8 attention using Hopper's FP8 WGMMA instructions, achieving close to 1.2 PFLOPs/s on H100. Two challenges addressed: (1) Layout conformance: FP8 WGMMA requires k-major operands, but V is head-dimension-major; FA3 performs in-kernel transpose of V tiles using LDSM/STSM instructions. (2) Accuracy: FP8 E4M3 has only 3 mantissa bits; FA3 uses block quantization (one scale per Br×d or Bc×d block) and incoherent processing (multiplying Q,K by random orthogonal M to reduce outliers) for 2.6× lower numerical error vs. per-tensor FP8.
keyFacts: FP8 WGMMA doubles matmul throughput vs FP16: ~2000 TFLOPs vs ~1000 TFLOPs on H100
FP8 E4M3 format: 1 sign + 4 exponent + 3 mantissa bits; max value 448; better for weights/activations
Block quantization: one scale per Br×d block of Q/K/V (vs per-tensor scaling) — fused with rotary embedding
Incoherent processing: Q' = QM, K' = KM where M is random orthogonal; (QM)(KM)^T = QK^T; spreads outliers
FP8 FA3 achieves 2.6× lower error than standard FP8 attention with per-tensor quantization
commonMisconceptions: FP8 attention is simply using FP8 in existing FA2 — requires significant algorithm changes for layout conformance and accuracy
Block quantization adds overhead — it is fused with preceding operations (e.g., rotary embedding) at no extra cost
FP8 accuracy is always worse than FP16 — with block quantization + incoherent processing, FA3 FP8 matches FP16 quality

concepts.title: Column/Row Parallel Linear Layers
concepts.preciseDefinition: Megatron-LM (Shoeybi et al. 2019, §3) implements intra-layer model parallelism by splitting transformer weight matrices across GPUs. For the MLP's first linear layer (Y = GeLU(XA)): A is split column-wise A = [A1, A2], so each GPU computes [GeLU(XA1), GeLU(XA2)] independently without synchronization. The second linear layer is split row-wise B = [B1; B2], taking the column-parallel output directly; an all-reduce then combines partial sums across GPUs. Self-attention is split per head: Q, K, V projections are column-parallel (each GPU handles some heads), and the output projection is row-parallel.
keyFacts: Column-parallel: weight A split as [A1, A2]; no sync needed before non-linear (GeLU can be applied per-shard)
Row-parallel: weight B split as [B1; B2]; inputs are sharded column results; one all-reduce in forward pass
Self-attention: Q/K/V projections column-parallel (each GPU handles heads_per_gpu heads); output proj row-parallel
Only 2 all-reduces per transformer layer in forward pass (g operator); 2 in backward pass (f operator)
Scales to 8-way model parallelism with 76% efficiency on 512 V100 GPUs; tested up to 8.3B parameters
commonMisconceptions: Tensor parallelism and pipeline parallelism are the same — tensor parallelism splits within a layer; pipeline parallelism splits across layers
All-reduce must happen at every layer boundary — Megatron fuses two GEMMs to reduce to one all-reduce per block
Tensor parallelism works well across nodes — all-reduce bottleneck limits tensor parallelism to within a node (NVLink bandwidth)

concepts.title: f and g Conjugate All-Reduce Operators
concepts.preciseDefinition: Megatron-LM (§3) introduces f and g as conjugate PyTorch autograd functions to cleanly implement the all-reduce placement in tensor-parallel layers. f is an identity in the forward pass and an all-reduce in the backward pass (for the first linear in a column-parallel pair). g is an all-reduce in the forward pass and identity in the backward pass (for the final linear in a row-parallel pair). Together they place the all-reduces correctly at the boundary of the model-parallel region without code changes to the rest of the model.
keyFacts: f forward: identity; f backward: all-reduce(gradient) — used at start of model-parallel region (column-parallel input)
g forward: all-reduce; g backward: identity — used at end of model-parallel region (row-parallel output)
f and g are conjugates: the all-reduce of one corresponds to identity of the other
Implementation: 10 lines of PyTorch autograd code — no custom C++ or compiler needed
A transformer layer uses exactly 4 all-reduces total (2 for MLP, 2 for self-attention) when using f/g correctly
commonMisconceptions: f and g are special hardware operations — they are simple PyTorch autograd functions inserting collective communication
All-reduce happens in both forward and backward at every placement — f places all-reduce only in backward; g only in forward
This pattern requires rewriting model code — Megatron inserts f and g at only 4 points in the transformer block

exercises.title: Column-Parallel and Row-Parallel Linear Layers
exercises.whatItTeaches: How to split a weight matrix column-wise for the first linear and row-wise for the second; implementing the f/g identity/all-reduce conjugate operators; verifying that col-parallel then row-parallel GEMM + one all-reduce produces the same output as a single large GEMM.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // Col-parallel: W = [W1 | W2]; each GPU: y_local = X @ W_local; // Row-parallel: W = [W1; W2]; each GPU: y_partial = X_local @ W_local; y = all_reduce(y_partial); // f operator: identity fwd, allreduce bwd
exercises.testCaseHint: Verify Y_tp == Y_full for 2-way split at hidden=1024; measure communication overhead with nccl collective timing

concepts.title: ZeRO Stage 1, 2, and 3 Memory Partitioning
concepts.preciseDefinition: ZeRO (Rajbhandari et al. 2020, §5) is a data-parallel optimization that eliminates memory redundancy by partitioning model states across Nd GPUs instead of replicating them. Mixed-precision Adam training of a model with Ψ parameters requires 16Ψ bytes (2Ψ fp16 params + 2Ψ fp16 grads + 4Ψ fp32 params + 4Ψ momentum + 4Ψ variance). Stage 1 (Pos): partitions optimizer state only → 4x memory reduction. Stage 2 (Pos+g): also partitions gradients → 8x reduction. Stage 3 (Pos+g+p): also partitions parameters → Nd-fold reduction (64 GPUs → 64× = linear scaling). Communication overhead: Stage 1/2 same as baseline DP; Stage 3 adds 50% extra communication.
keyFacts: Stage 1: each GPU stores KΨ/Nd optimizer state bytes; uses all-gather at step end to get updated params
Stage 2: each GPU stores (2Ψ + 14Ψ/Nd) bytes; gradients reduced via reduce-scatter to responsible partition
Stage 3: each GPU stores (16Ψ/Nd) bytes; all-gather params before forward/backward; release after
For 7.5B param model (K=12, Nd=64): Stage 0: 120 GB; Stage 1: 31.4 GB; Stage 2: 16.6 GB; Stage 3: 1.9 GB
ZeRO enables training 100B+ parameter models on commodity GPU clusters; DeepSpeed implements ZeRO
commonMisconceptions: ZeRO and model parallelism (tensor/pipeline) are alternatives — ZeRO is a data parallelism variant; can be combined with MP
Stage 3 always has 50% communication overhead — overhead is relative to baseline DP all-reduce; often amortized
ZeRO requires model code changes — ZeRO is transparent to model code (implemented in optimizer/DDP wrapper)

concepts.title: ZeRO Communication Volume Analysis
concepts.preciseDefinition: ZeRO-DP (§7) maintains the same asymptotic communication volume as standard data parallelism for Stages 1 and 2. Standard DP all-reduces each gradient (2Ψ elements per step). ZeRO Stage 1/2 replace all-reduce with reduce-scatter + all-gather which total the same 2Ψ elements. Stage 3 adds one all-gather for parameters before forward and one reduce-scatter for gradients: 3× Ψ communication per step vs 2× for standard DP — a 50% increase. This 50% overhead is the cost of Nd-fold memory reduction.
keyFacts: Standard DP: all-reduce = reduce-scatter + all-gather = 2Ψ elements communicated per step
ZeRO Stage 1+2: reduce-scatter (gradients) + all-gather (params) = 2Ψ — identical to standard DP
ZeRO Stage 3: all-gather (forward params) + reduce-scatter (backward grads) + all-gather (updated params) = 3Ψ
Super-linear speedup observed at 64-400 GPUs: larger DP degree allows larger batch per GPU, improving compute/communication ratio
ZeRO+MP: combine for max efficiency; MP within a node (NVLink), ZeRO-DP across nodes (InfiniBand)
commonMisconceptions: ZeRO Stage 3 doubles communication vs standard DP — it only adds 50% overhead (3Ψ vs 2Ψ)
Communication volume is the same as compute cost — communication cost depends on network bandwidth; compute cost on GPU FLOPs
ZeRO-R (activation optimization) affects the same communication — ZeRO-R manages activations separately from ZeRO-DP

coverageGaps: Speculative decoding kernels — complex, referenced in cuda-mode-lectures lecture_022; not planned
PagedAttention / vLLM KV cache management — important for production inference; no exercise planned
All-Reduce kernels (NCCL) — present in llm.c/dev/cuda/nccl_all_reduce.cu; not covered in single-GPU curriculum
Pipeline parallelism (GPipe, PipeDream) — mentioned in ZeRO paper as complementary; not in scope for single-GPU curriculum
Mamba / state-space model kernels — growing importance; not in current resources
